abstract:
The rapid evolution of generative pre-trained transformers and large language models has raised significant concerns regarding the integrity of academic work, sparking a global search for reliable methods to identify AI-generated content. This study investigates the efficacy of current AI detection tools by evaluating their accuracy and analyzing the nature of their errors. Specifically, we explore whether these tools can consistently distinguish between human-authored text and content generated by ChatGPT, and assess the degree to which machine translation and manual obfuscation techniques undermine their detection capabilities.

Our evaluation encompasses fourteen platforms, including twelve publicly available tools and two prominent commercial systems used in higher education, Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck. The findings indicate that current detection technologies are neither sufficiently accurate nor reliable for high-stakes academic application. We observed a systemic bias toward classifying AI-generated output as human-written, a problem that is exacerbated when content is intentionally obfuscated.

This research contributes to the field in three primary ways: first, it provides a comprehensive synthesis of contemporary scientific and industry efforts to track AI content; second, it presents a rigorous large-scale evaluation using an original dataset and a diverse array of tools; and finally, it offers a critical discussion on the ethical and practical limitations of relying on such software within an academic environment.
